essay_text:
It's December 2018. I have a calculus midterm in a week, but as I walk into the library, I am not here to
study. I descend to the familiar small, dimly-lit room in the basement. I rearrange tables, set up the
projector, and distribute laptops. I anxiously rummage through my notes as I wait for the girls to trickle
into the room. The first girl enters, clinging to her mom. Then other girls arrive, likewise, hiding behind
their mothers, as I try to encourage mingling amongst the group. I attempt to conceal my anxiousness
W
with a facade of hyper-enthusiasm. I move towards the podium, and while I have often taken my place in
front of it, today I stand behind it, muster my brightest smile and begin to teach my first computer science
class.
Coding is the metaphysical barrier between the outside world and virtual world. On my own, coding allows
E
me to be in my "bubble." Using the same loops and conditionals I teach about, I am able to transcend one
world and enter another. Yet when I give myself the task of presenting these same coding principles to the
young girls before me, my bubble collapses, and I exit my comfort zone. Nevertheless, I feel compelled to
teach coding classes to as many girls as I can reach to instill in them the confidence that coding can
I
generate within oneself, and the subsequent empowerment that can draw more young women into
technology. V
My desire to host coding classes has been brewing for years. It is a perfect medley of my quest for
opportunities to serve my community and my passion for computer science. My emphasis on specifically
E
teaching girls stems from a recognition of the lack of girls participating in tech-related endeavors. In High
School, I have taken computer science classes with three or fewer girls, and in robotics, I have entered
competitions flooded with all-boys teams while struggling to find all-girls teams other than my own. As my
team advances from our hometown competitions to regional competitions, to World Championships with
R
40,000 attendees, I am perplexed as girls in robotics are only speckled through the sea of boys competing.
This lack of representation fuels my fire to host more workshops and have an impact upon younger girls
so they will see themselves participating within future teams.
P
At times, I have felt the need to prove my worth to others, as if being a girl implies inferiority. Through
robotics, I have not only learned how to approach real-world problems with innovative engineering
solutions, but also to be an advocate for myself and others. For me, I refuse to be complicit in
perpetuating the gender stereotype that I have endured. I know that not every girl will want to join
robotics, or even continue to learn computer science, and that's okay. At the very least, I want to
emphasize that despite any barrier, coding is something they can do; if my all-girls team can compete our
way to World Championships, they can accomplish whatever they choose to pursue.
It's June 2019. I have become familiar with the basements of many libraries across Long Island, and today
is my latest stop. I descend to a modestly sized room with fluorescent lighting. I iterate through my
routine: introduce the coding concept of that day's lesson, run through the curriculum I have meticulously
prepared down to each minute, field any questions, provide guidance, and talk to each girl. At the end of
the class, when a mother and her daughter excitedly approach me to say that they have registered for
robotics, the hyper-enthusiasm I consistently exude is no longer a facade. My message has reached at
least one girl, encouraging me to reach the rest. With dozens of girls taught thus far, and more workshop
planning underway, I am confident that my efforts have only just begun.
8
prompt: Describe a problem you've solved or a problem you'd like to solve. It can be an intellectual challenge, a research query, an ethical dilemma-anything that is of personal importance, no matter the scale. Explain its significance to you and what steps you took or could be taken to identify a solution.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who crafts narratives that begin with vivid, sensory scenes and internal reflections, transitioning smoothly into broader themes and societal observations. You use personal anecdotes to ground abstract ideas, showing a clear progression from personal challenge to purposeful action. Maintain a tone that blends initial vulnerability with an evolving sense of determination and earnest conviction.
sentence_style: The sentence style is varied, ranging from concise, direct sentences for scene-setting to more complex, reflective structures for explaining deeper insights. The writer frequently employs parallelism and clear, logical transitions, creating a flowing and articulate rhythm. There's a balance between straightforward narration and nuanced expression.
vocabulary_level: Moderate to Sophisticated — Word choice is precise and often evocative, including terms like 'metaphysical,' 'transcend,' 'compelled,' 'perpetuating,' and 'meticulously,' indicating a thoughtful and articulate communicator without being overly academic.
structural_patterns: The essay uses a strong bookending or circular structure, opening and closing with specific dates and locations to illustrate personal growth over time. It relies on an anecdotal opening to draw the reader in, followed by personal reflection that expands into a problem-solution framework concerning societal issues. There's a clear progression from internal conflict to external impact.
distinctive_moves: Opening paragraphs with vivid, sensory scene-setting and immediate immersion in a specific moment.
Juxtaposing internal feelings (anxiety, discomfort) with external actions (hyper-enthusiasm, teaching) to show character development.
Employing metaphors (e.g., 'coding is the metaphysical barrier,' 'my bubble collapses') to explain complex ideas and personal experiences.
Directly addressing a societal issue (gender disparity in tech) with personal conviction and a call to action.
Showing clear progression and transformation from a 'facade' to genuine purpose and confidence.
themes_and_preoccupations: Gender equality and representation in STEM fields (computer science, robotics).
The power of mentorship and community service to inspire and empower others.
Overcoming personal anxieties and discomfort for a greater, altruistic purpose.
The journey of personal growth and confidence gained through leadership and advocacy.
self_presentation: The writer presents themselves as initially vulnerable and anxious, but quickly evolves into a confident, analytical, and deeply purposeful leader. They are an advocate who connects personal experiences of feeling 'inferiority' to a broader mission of empowering others, demonstrating humility alongside strong conviction.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: The essay is firmly rooted in the context of college application writing, focusing on personal growth, impact, and future aspirations. It references specific dates (2018, 2019), locations (library basements, Long Island), and activities (robotics competitions, World Championships) that ground the narrative in a contemporary STEM education and outreach environment.
avoid_in_imitation: Overly casual or colloquial language that would undermine the essay's thoughtful and earnest tone.
A purely descriptive narrative lacking internal reflection, personal growth, or connection to broader societal themes.
Exaggerated or boastful self-presentation; the confidence here is earned through demonstrated effort and vulnerability.
sample_sentence: Just as a carefully constructed algorithm can transform abstract data into tangible solutions, I've discovered that stepping beyond my own comfort zone clarifies my conviction to empower others.
